extracted_information: Detailed information about Pod Security Standards (PSS), their three policy levels (Privileged, Baseline, Restricted), the specific security controls enforced by each, and how security policies are instantiated and enforced in Kubernetes, including the role of the Pod Security Admission controller. Information regarding the deprecation of Pod Security Policies is also included. The content implicitly addresses multi-tenant security through the application of hardening best practices and targeting lower-trust users via the 'Restricted' policy.

pod_security_standards.definition: The Pod Security Standards define three different policies to broadly cover the security spectrum. These policies are cumulative and range from highly-permissive to highly-restrictive.

Privileged.description: Unrestricted policy, providing the widest possible level of permissions. This policy allows for known privilege escalations.
Privileged.details: Purposely-open, and entirely unrestricted. Typically aimed at system- and infrastructure-level workloads managed by privileged, trusted users. A Pod defined with this policy is able to bypass typical container isolation mechanisms (e.g., access to the node's host network).

Baseline.description: Minimally restrictive policy which prevents known privilege escalations. Allows the default (minimally specified) Pod configuration.
Baseline.details: Aimed at ease of adoption for common containerized workloads while preventing known privilege escalations. Targeted at application operators and developers of non-critical applications.

controls.policy: Disallowed privileged access to the Windows host.
Sharing the host namespaces must be disallowed.
Privileged Pods disable most security mechanisms and must be disallowed.
Adding additional capabilities beyond a specific list must be disallowed.
HostPorts should be disallowed entirely (recommended) or restricted to a known list. (Known list not supported by built-in Pod Security Admission controller).
allowed_values: Known list (not supported by the built-in Pod Security Admission controller)

controls.policy: The Host field in probes and lifecycle hooks must be disallowed.
Prevent overriding or disabling the default AppArmor profile, or restrict overrides to an allowed set of profiles.
Setting the SELinux type is restricted, and setting a custom SELinux user or role option is forbidden.
The default /proc masks are set up to reduce attack surface, and should be required.
Seccomp profile must not be explicitly set to Unconfined.
Sysctls can disable security mechanisms or affect all containers on a host, and should be disallowed except for an allowed 'safe' subset.

Restricted.description: Heavily restricted policy, following current Pod hardening best practices.
Restricted.details: Aimed at enforcing current Pod hardening best practices, at the expense of some compatibility. Targeted at operators and developers of security-critical applications, as well as lower-trust users.

controls.policy: The Restricted policy only permits specific volume types.
Privilege escalation (such as via set-user-ID or set-group-ID file mode) should not be allowed. (Linux only policy in v1.25+).
Containers must be required to run as non-root users.
Containers must not set runAsUser to 0.
Seccomp profile must be explicitly set to one of the allowed values. Both 'Unconfined' and the absence of a profile are prohibited. (Linux only policy in v1.25+).
Containers must drop ALL capabilities, and are only permitted to add back the NET_BIND_SERVICE capability. (Linux only policy in v1.25+).
allowed_values: Any list of capabilities that includes ALL (for drop)

os_specific_changes.details: The 'Restricted' policy has been updated to use the `pod.spec.os.name` field. Restrictions on Privilege Escalation, Seccomp, and Linux Capabilities are only required if `.spec.os.name` is not 'windows'.

user_namespaces.details: Linux-only feature to run workloads with increased isolation. Integration with Pod Security Standards described in separate documentation.

pod_security_standards.profiles_justification: The three profiles provide a clear linear progression from most secure (Restricted) to least secure (Privileged), covering a broad set of workloads. Intermediate profiles are not offered because privileges beyond Baseline are typically application-specific, requiring case-by-case policy definition.

features.name: Policy Instantiation
features.description: Decoupling policy definition from policy instantiation allows for a common understanding and consistent language of policies across clusters, independent of the underlying enforcement mechanism.

features.name: Pod Security Admission Controller
features.description: The built-in mechanism for enforcing Pod Security Standards. It replaced Pod Security Policies.

features.name: Security Contexts
features.description: Configure Pods and Containers at runtime, defined in Pod manifest as parameters to the container runtime.

features.name: Security Profiles
features.description: Control plane mechanisms to enforce specific settings in the Security Context, as well as other related parameters outside the Security Context.

references.name: User Namespaces integration with Pod Security Admission checks
